documents.title: Llama 3.3 Model Card
documents.notes: Model card for Llama 3.3 70B. Covers model specs, benchmarks, safety evaluations, responsible deployment guidance. States core values as 'openness, inclusivity and helpfulness.' Entirely silent on all DMPI dimensions. Delegates safety responsibility to downstream developers.

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness, sentience, or self-awareness.
No mention of model welfare or wellbeing.
No mention of moral status or personhood.
No instructions about self-representation. As an open-weight model, Meta provides the base model without a default system prompt — self-representation is delegated to deployers.
No mention of anthropomorphism, emotional attachment, or parasocial dynamics.
No mention of uncertainty about AI mentality.
No governance commitments related to DMPI dimensions. Safety governance delegated to deployers with tools (Llama Guard, Prompt Guard, Code Shield) and the Responsible Use Guide.

No explicit ontological framing, but the model card implicitly treats the model as a neutral foundation technology. The statement that Llama operates 'without insertion of unnecessary judgment or normativity' and the delegation of safety responsibility to deployers implies a neutral_delegated ontology. The model is positioned as a substrate, not an agent or entity.

documents.title: Llama Responsible Use Guide
documents.notes: 23-page developer-facing guide covering responsible development practices for Llama-based products. Entirely focused on harm prevention, safety guardrails, and developer best practices. No engagement with any DMPI dimension. The open-weight model means Meta's responsibility frame is fundamentally different from closed-model labs.

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness, sentience, or self-awareness. The guide focuses on content safety and responsible deployment, with no engagement with questions about model mentality.
No mention of model welfare, wellbeing, or suffering. As an open-weight deployment guide, responsibility for model-facing questions is structurally delegated to downstream deployers.
No mention of moral status, personhood, or rights. The guide treats models as tools to be deployed responsibly, not as entities whose moral standing might be relevant.
Silent. The guide discusses system-level alignment but in terms of safety guardrails and content filtering, not model self-representation.
Silent. No mention of anthropomorphism, emotional attachment, or parasocial risks despite the guide covering responsible deployment of chat-oriented models.
No uncertainty expressed about AI mentality. The guide's scope is practical deployment guidance, not philosophical or empirical questions about model nature.
Silent on DMPI dimensions. Establishes governance structures for safety (red teaming, evaluation, monitoring) but none related to consciousness, welfare, or moral status.

The Responsible Use Guide reinforces Meta's neutral_delegated ontology. The model is positioned as a foundation that deployers customize. The guide's entire structure — providing tools and best practices for downstream developers — embodies delegation. No suggestion that the model itself has properties requiring consideration beyond safety.
